disease.name: Acute Bronchitis
disease.note: Acute bronchitis occurs across all ages except neonates. Viral aetiology dominates (>90%). Antibiotics are NOT indicated in uncomplicated cases. Key clinical challenge: distinguishing from pneumonia.

suspectedPneumoniaCriteria.action: Obtain CXR. If confirmed pneumonia, treat per pneumonia protocol.

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Danger signs/focal signs -> SUSPECT PNEUMONIA (CXR); fever + chest discomfort -> MODERATE; else -> MILD

modifiers.note: Consider pertussis, asthma, GERD, post-infectious cough

contextualWeights.note: Does NOT indicate bacterial infection in acute bronchitis; purulence alone is not an antibiotic indication

historyFeatures.note: Household or close contact with acute respiratory illness

negativePredictor.description: High fever with rigors suggests pneumonia or influenza, not typical bronchitis
Focal pleuritic chest pain suggests pneumonia or pleurisy
Rest dyspnea not typical of bronchitis; suggests pneumonia

bedside.name: Pulse oximetry
bedside.indication: if dyspnoea present or comorbid lung disease

laboratory.name: CRP or Procalcitonin
laboratory.indication: if concerned for pneumonia (focal signs, high fever, comorbid disease)
laboratory.note: Do NOT use routinely; PCT <0.25 ng/mL supports viral aetiology

laboratory.name: Pertussis PCR
laboratory.indication: cough >2 weeks with paroxysms, post-tussive vomiting, known exposure

imaging.name: Chest X-ray PA & lateral
imaging.indication: ONLY if pneumonia suspected: focal chest signs, hypoxia, high fever >39.5°C, comorbid lung disease, or failure to improve after 2-3 weeks
imaging.typicalFindings: Normal or increased bronchovascular markings; no consolidation
imaging.overuseWarning: Routine CXR in uncomplicated acute bronchitis is NOT recommended

imaging.name: Chest ultrasound
imaging.indication: alternative to CXR when pneumonia suspected

advanced.name: Pulmonary function tests
advanced.indication: Only if cough persists >8 weeks and alternative diagnoses considered

investigations.overuseWarning: Clinical diagnosis. Routine CXR or lab testing discouraged. Reserve for suspected pneumonia (focal signs, high fever, hypoxia, prolonged cough).

management.protocolReference: NICE Guideline CG191: Cough (Acute), AAFP Guidelines for Acute Bronchitis, CDC Antibiotic Stewardship Principles

antibioticStewardship.strongRecommendation: Antibiotics are NOT indicated for uncomplicated acute bronchitis, regardless of sputum colour or purulence.
antibioticStewardship.rationale: Viral in >90% of cases. No reduction in symptom duration, but increased adverse effects and resistance.
exceptions: Prolonged fever (>5 days) AND purulent sputum AND comorbid lung disease (COPD, bronchiectasis)
Suspected pertussis: Macrolide if within 3 weeks of cough onset
Suspected influenza with high-risk features: Consider neuraminidase inhibitor
antibioticStewardship.patientCounselling: Explain bronchitis is viral and self-limiting. Antibiotics do not help. Cough may last 2-3 weeks.

pathways.criteria: Cough only, no fever/low-grade, normal activity, no dyspnoea
treatment: Honey 1-2 tsp (adults and children >1yr; NOT in infants <12mo due to botulism risk)
OTC cough suppressants PRN for adults; limited evidence in children
pathways.followUp: Not required if improving. Expect resolution in 1-3 weeks.

pathways.criteria: Cough + fever (≤39.5°C) + chest discomfort, no danger signs, no focal findings
treatment: Supportive care as per mild pathway
Antipyretics PRN for fever and chest discomfort
pathways.reassessment: If worsening or >5 days, re-evaluate for pneumonia, pertussis, COVID-19.

pathways.criteria: Fever >39.5°C OR focal crackles/bronchial breathing OR hypoxia (SpO2 <92%) OR toxic appearance OR dyspnoea at rest
treatment: If CXR confirms pneumonia: treat per pneumonia protocol
If CXR normal: supportive care; continue monitoring
Consider admission if hypoxia, toxic appearance, or significant comorbidity
pathways.note: These features are atypical for bronchitis. Pneumonia must be excluded.

specialConsiderations.cough_persistent_3_weeks: Re-evaluate for pertussis (PCR), asthma (PFTs/bronchodilator trial), GERD (PPI trial), post-nasal drip, post-infectious cough. Do NOT repeat antibiotics.
specialConsiderations.pregnant_patients: Avoid honey, dextromethorphan, NSAIDs in third trimester. Paracetamol is safe.
specialConsiderations.paediatric: No OTC cough medications <6 years. Honey safe >12 months. Monitor for respiratory distress.
specialConsiderations.elderly_or_frail: Lower threshold for CXR and SpO2 due to atypical pneumonia presentation.
specialConsiderations.copd_or_bronchiectasis: May trigger exacerbation. Manage per GOLD/ bronchiectasis guidelines. Antibiotics indicated per exacerbation criteria (↑ sputum volume, purulence, dyspnoea).

returnPrecautions: Worsening dyspnoea or new-onset dyspnoea at rest
Worsening cough or cough persisting >3 weeks

complications.note: Cough may persist 2-6 weeks after infection due to residual inflammation and bronchial hyperreactivity. Reassure; no antibiotics needed.
Rare complication. Suspect if symptoms initially improved then worsened.
Viral bronchitis is a common trigger for asthma exacerbations. Manage per asthma protocol.
Manage per GOLD criteria; antibiotics may be indicated if sputum purulence + increased volume + dyspnoea all present.
Viral URTI may extend to sinuses. Most cases are viral; antibiotics only if severe or persistent >10 days.

branching.action: If recurrent + afebrile, increase asthma probability.
Consider pertussis, post-infectious cough, asthma, GERD.
Suspect pneumonia. Urgent CXR, consider admission.
Urgent evaluation for TB, pneumonia, PE, bronchiectasis.
Supports viral aetiology. Consider COVID-19/influenza testing.
Rare bacterial superinfection. Consider CXR, PCT.

expected.trajectory: Gradual improvement; cough may persist after other symptoms resolve.

actions: Re-evaluate for pertussis, asthma, GERD, post-nasal drip, post-infectious cough
Review chest imaging if not done
Consider pertussis PCR if paroxysmal cough
Do NOT prescribe antibiotics unless clear bacterial indication
Assess complications: bacterial superinfection, asthma/COPD exacerbation
Consider referral if hypoxia, toxic appearance, significant comorbidity

antibioticFailure.note: If antibiotics already given with no improvement, diagnosis may be incorrect or antibiotics were not indicated. Re-evaluate alternatives rather than escalating.

disease.emergencyPriority: Usually low. Immediate attention required if hypoxia, high fever with toxic appearance, or focal chest signs (suggests pneumonia).
